papers.title: Gaia and the Galactic Center Origin of Hypervelocity Stars
papers.author_names: Warren R. Brown Mario G. Lattanzi Scott J. Kenyon Margaret J. Geller

abstract.text: We use new Gaia measurements to explore the origin of the highest velocity stars in the Hypervelocity Star Survey. The measurements reveal a clear pattern in the B-type stars. Halo stars dominate the sample at speeds about 100 km/s below Galactic escape velocity. Disk runaway stars have speeds up to 100 km/s above Galactic escape velocity, but most disk runaways are bound. Stars with speeds about 100 km/s above Galactic escape velocity originate from the Galactic center. Two bound stars may also originate from the Galactic center. Future Gaia measurements will enable a large, clean sample of Galactic center ejections for measuring the massive black hole ejection rate of hypervelocity stars, and for constraining the mass distribution of the Milky Way dark matter halo.

brief.tldr: [research paper] Gaia's high-precision proper motion measurements reveal that stars with velocities >100 km s⁻¹ above Galactic escape velocity originate from the Galactic center, supporting Hills' 1988 model of hypervelocity star ejection via three-body interactions with a massive black hole. Halo stars dominate at velocities below escape velocity, while disk runaways are mostly bound; two bound stars also show Galactic center origin, indicating a significant ejection rate from the central region. The data enable direct trajectory-based origin classification and constrain the mass distribution of the Milky Way's dark matter halo.

source_fields.title: Gaia and the Galactic Center Origin of Hypervelocity Stars

source_fields.sections_text: Data: This section presents a sample of 42 hypervelocity stars with radial velocities greater than +275 km s⁻¹, selected from the HVS Survey, and includes proper motion data from Gaia for 39 of them, used to analyze their kinematics and origins in the Galactic halo. Analysis: This section describes the method for determining the origin of hypervelocity stars by simulating their backward trajectories through a Galactic gravitational potential model and estimating the ejection velocities required to produce their observed motions. Conclusions: Gaia proper motions enable the robust discrimination between Galactic center ejections and other high-velocity stars, highlighting the dominance of Galactic center HVSs at speeds above escape velocity and underscoring their potential to constrain the Milky Way's mass distribution and dynamics. Introduction: This paper makes the novel contribution of using Gaia's precise proper motion measurements to directly determine the origins of hypervelocity stars—distinguishing between Galactic center ejections, disk runaways, and halo stars—thereby solving the long-standing problem of unambiguously linking unbound stellar velocities to their formation mechanisms. Constraints on Origin: The analysis of velocity and trajectory data identifies Galactic center origins as statistically preferred for the fastest hypervelocity stars, with strong constraints against disk ejection and evidence for specific bound stars potentially originating from the Galactic center or halo.

abstract: We use new Gaia measurements to explore the origin of the highest velocity stars in the Hypervelocity Star Survey. The measurements reveal a clear pattern in the B-type stars.
Halo stars dominate the sample at speeds about 100 km/s below Galactic escape velocity. Disk runaway stars have speeds up to 100 km/s above Galactic escape velocity, but most disk runaways are bound.
Stars with speeds about 100 km/s above Galactic escape velocity originate from the Galactic center. Two bound stars may also originate from the Galactic center.

Future Gaia measurements will enable a large, clean sample of Galactic center ejections for measuring the massive black hole ejection rate of hypervelocity stars, and for constraining the mass distribution
title: Gaia and the Galactic Center Origin of Hypervelocity Stars

catalog_assessment.evidence: Uses Gaia measurements to analyze stars from the Hypervelocity Star Survey, classifying origins based on kinematics. Abstract mentions 'sample' and discusses specific velocity groups.

papers.title: Old, Metal-Poor Extreme Velocity Stars in the Solar Neighborhood
papers.author_names: Kohei Hattori Monica Valluri Eric F. Bell Ian U. Roederer

abstract.text: We report the discovery of 30 stars with extreme space velocities ($>$ 480 km/s) in the Gaia-DR2 archive. These stars are a subset of 1743 stars with high-precision parallax, large tangential velocity ($v_{tan}>$ 300 km/s), and measured line-of-sight velocity in DR2. By tracing the orbits of the stars back in time, we find at least one of them is consistent with having been ejected by the supermassive black hole at the Galactic Center. Another star has an orbit that passed near the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) about 200 Myr ago. Unlike previously discovered blue hypervelocity stars, our sample is metal-poor (-1.5 $<$ [Fe/H] $<$ -1.0) and quite old ($>$ 1 Gyr). We discuss possible mechanisms for accelerating old stars to such extreme velocities. The high observed space density of this population, relative to potential acceleration mechanisms, implies that these stars are probably bound to the Milky Way (MW). If they are bound, the discovery of this population would require a local escape speed of around $\\\\sim$ 600 km/s and consequently imply a virial mass of $M_{200} \\\\sim 1.4 \\\\times 10^{12} M_\\\\odot$ for the MW.

triage.label: rule-direct
triage.reason: Direct title match: high-velocity or extreme-velocity stars

brief.tldr: [research paper] The paper identifies 30 old, metal-poor stars with extreme space velocities (>480 km/s) in the solar neighborhood using Gaia-DR2 data, revealing at least one star likely ejected by the Galactic Center's supermassive black hole and another that passed near the Large Magellanic Cloud 200 Myr ago. These stars are distinct from known hypervelocity stars in age and metallicity, suggesting alternative ejection mechanisms; their high local space density implies they are bound to the Milky Way, requiring a local escape speed of ~600 km/s and implying a MW virial mass of ~1.4×10¹² M⊙.

source_fields.title: Old, Metal-Poor Extreme Velocity Stars in the Solar Neighborhood

source_fields.sections_text: Discussion: The extreme velocities of the stars in the sample (≥480 km s⁻¹) are unlikely to result from ejection by Type Ia supernova companions due to insufficient ejection velocities for giant stars, though a plausible mechanism involves binary companions of SN Ia in globular clusters (e.g., in the Milky Way or LMC) that were ejected when the cluster passed near apocenter, potentially achieving velocities up to ~500 km s⁻¹. Conclusions: We have identified 30 new extreme velocity stars that are old, metal-poor, and likely originated from the LMC, Galactic Center, or stellar halo, with implications for the Milky Way's dark matter mass and local escape velocity. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel approach to identifying and characterizing extreme velocity stars beyond young, massive binaries, addressing the unresolved problem of their origin by providing a comprehensive framework to test ejection mechanisms and constrain the Milky Way's mass through local kinematic measurements. Observed properties: The observed color-magnitude properties and spectroscopic data indicate that the extreme velocity stars in the sample are predominantly old and metal-poor giants with [Fe/H] between -1.5 and -1.0. Appendix C ADQL queries: This appendix provides ADQL queries to retrieve a sample of old, metal-poor stars with extreme radial velocities from Gaia DR2 and to assess selection biases by comparing number densities of stars with and without good parallax measurements. Appendix A Dust correction: This appendix details the method for deriving dust-corrected Gaia colors and magnitudes using extinction models and empirical relationships based on stellar position and observed colors. Sample Selection and Orbit Computation: This section describes the selection of 30 unbound "extreme velocity" stars from Gaia DR2 based on high-precision tangential velocities, accurate 3D kinematics, and orbital energy calculations in a Milky Way potential model, with the sample's properties robust to measurement uncertainties and selection methods. Appendix B Selection effect in high parallax-precision sample: The selection criterion of high-precision parallax ($\\varpi/\\delta\\varpi > 10$) introduces a sky asymmetry in the stellar sample, favoring high ecliptic latitudes where Gaia observes more frequently and disfavoring dense regions like the Galactic plane or the LMC.

abstract: We report the discovery of 30 stars with extreme space velocities ($>$ 480 km/s) in the Gaia-DR2 archive.
These stars are a subset of 1743 stars with high-precision parallax, large tangential velocity ($v_{tan}>$ 300 km/s), and measured line-of-sight velocity in DR2.
Unlike previously discovered blue hypervelocity stars, our sample is metal-poor (-1.5 $<$ [Fe/H] $<$ -1.0) and quite old ($>$ 1 Gyr).
We discuss possible mechanisms for accelerating old stars to such extreme velocities.
The high observed space density of this population, relative to potential acceleration mechanisms, implies that these stars are probably bound to the Milky Way (MW).
title: Old, Metal-Poor Extreme Velocity Stars in the Solar Neighborhood

catalog_assessment.evidence: Reports discovery of 30 extreme velocity stars from Gaia-DR2, subset of 1743 high-precision stars. Provides orbital analysis and discusses ejection mechanisms.

papers.title: LP 543-25: a rare low-mass runaway disk star
papers.author_names: R. de la Fuente Marcos C. de la Fuente Marcos

abstract.text: LP 543-25 or PSS 544-7 is a high proper-motion star located 458 pc from the Sun in the constellation of Canis Minor; it has been argued that it could be a candidate cannonball star ejected by a star cluster. Here, we revisit the issue of the kinematics of this interesting star using Gaia DR2. The heliocentric Galactic velocity components are (U, V, W) = (206, -289, 30) km/s; the corresponding Galactocentric Galactic velocity components show that LP 543-25 is moving in the Galactic plane and away from the Galactic Center at a rate of nearly 200 km/s, which is compatible with an origin in one of the multiple star clusters that inhabit the inner regions of the Milky Way. LP 543-25 appears to be a member of an elusive class of stars, the low-mass runaway stars. It is perhaps one of the closest and less massive runaway stars identified so far.

triage.label: rule-weak-llm-confirmed
triage.reason: Weak title match: runaway-related mechanisms LLM confirmed: Title explicitly mentions 'runaway disk star', abstract discusses high proper motion, ejection from star cluster, and classifies it as a low-mass runaway star with high velocity components.
